Campus elections rely on a delegate-based system
Delegates vote on behalf of the student body
Results do not always reflect student opinion
Long physical queues reduce voter participation
No independent way to verify votes
There is a clear need for a
where every student votes from their device — eliminating queues and producing instant, trustworthy results.
AIM: Design and implement a Secure & Verifiable Student E-Voting System that allows all students to vote independently, quickly, and securely.
shortcomings of the current voting approach

Recommend integration strategies for campus elections
Introduction · Related Systems · Limitations · Proposed Solutions

This chapter surveys published work on electronic voting systems, providing critical context for the proposed solution. Existing implementations are examined to identify their strengths, limitations, and the research gap this project addresses (Gritzalis, 2002).

Three categories are reviewed: paper-based voting, electronic voting machines (EVMs), and online/web-based platforms. Each is evaluated on security, transparency, accessibility, and cost (Alvarez & Hall, 2008).
Sources: peer-reviewed journals, conference proceedings, and technical reports (2015-2024) following Kitchenham’s (2004) systematic review methodology.
1.  What e-voting systems exist and how do they work?
2.  What are the documented limitations of these systems?
3.  What gap justifies a campus-specific solution?
4.  How can cryptography improve vote security and verifiability?
“Security and verifiability are the two pillars upon which any credible e-voting system must be built” (Chaum et al., 2008, p. 14).
Web-based, open-source e-voting system using homomorphic encryption.
Helios allows voters to verify their ballots were correctly counted without revealing their vote. Used for university and professional association elections (Adida, 2008).
Adida, B. (2008). Helios: Web-based open-audit voting. USENIX Security Symposium, 8, 335-348.
Commercial cloud-based voting platform used in organisational elections.
Polyas is deployed across Europe for student, corporate, and government elections, offering SSL-secured voting with multi-factor authentication (Polyas, 2022).
Polyas GmbH. (2022). Online voting system white paper. https://www.polyas.com
State-run internet voting system for NSW elections in Australia.
iVote enabled remote voting for electors with disabilities. Security researchers later identified critical vulnerabilities in its authentication mechanism (Springall et al., 2014).
Springall, D., et al. (2014). Security analysis of the Estonian internet voting system. ACM CCS, pp. 703-715.
2.3  Limitations and Weaknesses of Existing Systems
Limitation Category          Paper-Based Voting          Electronic Voting Machines          Online Systems
Ballot stuffing and tampering risk (Alvarez & Hall, 2008)          Hardware vulnerabilities; limited audit trails          Susceptible to MITM attacks and server breaches (Springall et al., 2014)
Manual count is opaque; error-prone (Mercuri, 2002)          Closed-source firmware; voters cannot verify          Results require trust in server operator; no independent audit
Physical presence required; long queues (Gritzalis, 2002)          Fixed polling stations; high deployment cost          Requires reliable internet; excludes low-bandwidth users
No individual verification mechanism          No voter receipt or confirmation system          Partial — Helios provides receipts; most commercial systems do not (Adida, 2008)
Low — delegate proxy model excludes direct participation          Low — prohibitive hardware cost for small elections          Medium — potential, but none designed for campus-scale direct voting
2.4  How the Proposed Solution Addresses These Weaknesses
Existing systems lack strong cryptographic protection, leaving votes vulnerable to interception and manipulation (Springall et al., 2014).
AES-256 encryption for every ballot at rest; RSA key exchange for transmission. Each vote is encrypted before database storage.
Most systems require blind trust in administrators; results cannot be independently verified by voters (Mercuri, 2002).
Tamper-evident audit logs and unique voter confirmation codes allow each student to verify their ballot was correctly recorded.
Physical queues and rigid polling hours discourage participation, reducing representativeness of results (Gritzalis, 2002).
Students vote from any device at any time during the polling window. No physical presence required; instant automated tallying.
Delegate-proxy models mean most students never cast a direct vote, creating a democratic deficit in campus governance (Alvarez & Hall, 2008).
Every registered student is a direct voter. One student, one verified, encrypted vote — no intermediary delegate required.
No system combines speed, security, and direct participation for campus use
Existing online systems lack strong cryptographic vote protection
None provide students individual vote verification
No solution specifically addresses the queue problem in small-scale elections
Agile Development · System Design · Workflow · Tools · Security

This chapter describes how the research will be carried out to meet the project objectives. It details the chosen development methodology, research design, machine learning components, and data collection strategy.

An applied, design-science approach is used, combining iterative software development with empirical evaluation. The study is both constructive (building a system) and evaluative (testing its effectiveness).
Hevner et al. (2004) define design science as creating and evaluating IT artefacts intended to solve identified organisational problems.
3.2  Methodology — Agile SDLC and ML algorithm selection, justification, and research design.
3.3  Data Collection — Methods, tools, datasets, and ethical considerations.
3.4  System Design — Architecture, components, and security mechanisms.
“A methodology is not simply a collection of methods but a theoretically grounded framework that guides the entire research process” (Creswell, 2014, p. 3).
Agile (Scrum framework) is selected for this project. It supports iterative delivery, continuous feedback, and adaptive planning — essential for a security-critical system (Beck et al., 2001).
• Short sprints expose security flaws early
• User stories ground features in real student needs
• Sprint reviews allow continuous evaluation against objectives
• More flexible than Waterfall for evolving security requirements
Beck, K., et al. (2001). Manifesto for agile software development. Agile Alliance. https://agilemanifesto.org
Structured survey distributed to 150 students measuring satisfaction with current voting, usability expectations, and security awareness.
User Acceptance Testing with 30 student volunteers across 3 sprint cycles to validate interface and system correctness.
Ethics: informed consent obtained; data anonymised per university policy.

Secure login using student credentials; prevents duplicate access
Clean, intuitive UI accessible from any device or browser
Encrypts each vote before storage; ensures confidentiality
Automatically tallies votes; produces real-time results
Secure storage of encrypted votes and audit logs
Student logs in using campus credentials
Student views candidates and casts vote
Vote is encrypted and stored in the database
Unique confirmation code generated for student
Every ballot is encrypted before storage — unreadable without the decryption key
Student ID + password ensures only registered students can access the system
System blocks any attempt to vote more than once per election
Tamper-evident logs record all system events for post-election review
Each voter receives a unique hash to verify their vote was recorded
Database access controlled by role-based permissions and encryption at rest
Shows external entities interacting with the current manual/delegate-based voting system.
Voter Marks Ballot & Casts Into Box
Students register with institutional credentials; login via username/password + OTP (MFA).
Admins create, schedule, open and close elections; manage candidate lists.
Authenticated voters select one candidate; vote encrypted before submission.
Each voter receives a unique hash code to verify their vote was recorded.
System enforces strict one-voter-one-vote; duplicate attempts blocked & logged.
System tallies encrypted votes after election closes; publishes auditable results.
Voters verify via unique hash code
A web-based, server-side secure e-voting platform using standard cryptographic techniques:
Username + password + OTP second factor. Role-based access for voters vs admins.
All votes encrypted (AES-256) in transit and at rest. HTTPS enforced throughout.
SHA-256 hash generated per vote. Voters receive a personal verification token.
Hash-chained, append-only audit log with Merkle tree structure for tamper detection.
Each user's voting status flagged in DB. System blocks re-submission at every layer.
Auto session timeout after inactivity. Redundant storage. 99.9% uptime target.
«include» relationships exist between Login → OTP Verification and Cast Vote → Vote Encryption.
Key classes, attributes, methods, and relationships
Step-by-step flow from login to vote submission
5.2  Database Design – Key Strategies & Cryptographic Storage
VOTES table is INSERT-only — no UPDATE or DELETE allowed
Database triggers enforce immutability at DB level
Any attempt to modify past votes raises an exception
Each audit log row stores SHA-256 of the previous row
Breaking the chain (modifying any row) immediately detectable
Merkle root computed and published after every election
Votes encrypted with AES-256 before DB insert
RSA-2048 used to encrypt the AES session key
Encryption keys stored in a separate Key Management Service
Master keys stored separately from vote database
Role-based key access — admin cannot read votes directly
Key rotation policy enforced between election cycles
Admin Dashboard also includes: Election management  ·  Real-time vote tallies  ·  Full audit log viewer  ·  User role management
5.3  User Interface Design – Admin Dashboard & Results View
Description of the Development Environment and Testing Procedures
The backend was developed using Python and the Flask framework, following a REST API architecture.
Backend uses Flask REST APIs and implements multiple cryptographic layers for end-to-end vote security.
The frontend was developed using HTML, CSS, and Vanilla JavaScript to create a lightweight and responsive user interface.
Overall, the system follows a three-tier architecture: Presentation Layer  ·  Application Layer  ·  Data Layer
Test Data   ·   Test Cases   ·   Test Results
Test data included sample users (admin and student accounts), sample elections, and multiple candidates across positions.
Duplicate vote attempts using same student account
Tamper attempts via direct SQL UPDATE commands on vote records
Manual modification of audit log entries to test tamper detection
Admin/student role cross-access attempts on restricted endpoints
User logs in successfully; session created
Vote stored in DB; unique hash generated per ballot
Same student attempts a second vote
System blocks attempt; error message returned
Data stored as AES-GCM ciphertext — unreadable in plaintext
SQL UPDATE attempted directly on votes table
Operation blocked; vote record unchanged and protected
Student submits vote hash for verification
System confirms vote is recorded and unaltered
System detects broken hash chain; flags tampering
Admin attempts to access student-only verify endpoint
Access denied for admin; student access permitted
All critical functional requirements were tested successfully. The system correctly enforced security constraints, prevented unauthorized actions, and detected tampering attempts.
Testing was not limited to functionality — it also included security validation: encryption, immutability, and tamper detection.
